topic: Economic Objectives and the Role of Government
subject: economics

items.q: How is economic growth shown on a Production Possibility Curve?
items.correct: By an outward shift of the entire curve — reflecting an increase in productive capacity through more or better resources, technology, or skills.
items.misconception: Growth is shown by moving from inside the curve to a point on the curve.
items.random_wrong: Growth is shown by the curve becoming straighter.
items.why: Moving from inside to on the curve is RECOVERY (using existing resources better), not growth. True growth requires the curve to shift outward — students confuse recovery from inefficiency with new capacity.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: Changes to interest rates in the UK are decided by:
items.correct: MPC of the Bank of England
items.why: It's MPC of the Bank of England. “HM Treasury” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is a fiscal policy tool?
items.why: It's Income tax. “Interest rates” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many members does the Bank of England MPC have?
items.why: Exact answer: 9 members. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
